elements.sent-2411-003: These instructions give you guidelines for preparing papers for IEEE Transactions and Journals.
elements.sent-2411-004: Use this document as a template if you are using \LaTeX.
elements.sent-2411-005: Otherwise, use this document as an instruction set.
elements.sent-2411-006: The electronic file of your paper will be formatted further at IEEE.
elements.sent-2411-007: Paper titles should be written in uppercase and lowercase letters, not all uppercase.
elements.sent-2411-008: Avoid writing long formulas with subscripts in the title; short formulas that identify the elements are fine (e.g., "Nd--Fe--B").
elements.sent-2411-009: Do not write ``(Invited)'' in the title.
elements.sent-2411-010: Full names of authors are preferred in the author field, but are not required.
elements.sent-2411-011: Put a space between authors' initials.
elements.sent-2411-012: The abstract must be a concise yet comprehensive reflection of what is in your article.
elements.sent-2411-013: In particular, the abstract must be self-contained, without abbreviations, footnotes, or references.
elements.sent-2411-014: It should be a microcosm of the full article.
elements.sent-2411-015: The abstract must be between 150--250 words.
elements.sent-2411-016: Be sure that you adhere to these limits; otherwise, you will need to edit your abstract accordingly.
elements.sent-2411-017: The abstract must be written as one paragraph, and should not contain displayed mathematical equations or tabular material.
elements.sent-2411-018: The abstract should include three or four different keywords or phrases, as this will help readers to find it.
elements.sent-2411-019: It is important to avoid over-repetition of such phrases as this can result in a page being rejected by search engines.
elements.sent-2411-020: Ensure that your abstract reads well and is grammatically correct.
elements.sent-2411-021: For a list of suggested keywords, send a blank e-mail to keywords@ieee.org or visit \underline {http://www.ieee.org/\discretionary{ { { organizations/\discretionary{ { { pubs/\discretionary{ { { ani\_prod/}\discretionary{ { {}\underline {keywrd98.txt \end{IEEEkeywords.}}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-022: If you are reading a paper or PDF version of this document, please download the electronic file, trans\_jour.tex, from the IEEE Web site at \underline {http://www.ieee.org/authortools/trans\_jour.tex so you can use it to prepare your manuscript.}
elements.sent-2411-023: If you would prefer to use LaTeX, download IEEE's LaTeX style and sample files from the same Web page.
elements.sent-2411-024: You can also explore using the Overleaf editor at \underline {https://www.overleaf.com/blog/278-how-to-use-overleaf-with-}\discretionary{ { {}\underline {ieee-collabratec-your-quick-guide-to-getting-started\#.}\discretionary{ { { xsVp6tpPkrKM9 If your paper is intended for a conference, please contact your conference editor concerning acceptable word processor formats for your particular conference.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-025: IEEE will do the final formatting of your paper.
elements.sent-2411-026: If your paper is intended for a conference, please observe the conference page limits.
elements.sent-2411-027: Define abbreviations and acronyms the first time they are used in the text, even after they have already been defined in the abstract.
elements.sent-2411-028: Abbreviations such as IEEE, SI, ac, and dc do not have to be defined.
elements.sent-2411-029: Abbreviations that incorporate periods should not have spaces: write ``C.N.R.S.,'' not ``C.. N.
elements.sent-2411-030: S.'' Do not use abbreviations in the title unless they are unavoidable (for example, ``IEEE'' in the title of this article).
elements.sent-2411-031: Use one space after periods and colons.
elements.sent-2411-032: Hyphenate complex modifiers: ``zero-field-cooled magnetization.'' Avoid dangling participles, such as, ``Using \eqref{eq , the potential was calculated.'' [It is not clear who or what used \eqref{eq .] Write instead, ``The potential was calculated by using \eqref{eq ,'' or ``Using \eqref{eq , we calculated the potential.'' Use a zero before decimal points: ``0.25,'' not ``.25.'' Use ``cm$^{3}$,'' not ``cc.'' Indicate sample dimensions as ``0.1 cm $\times $ 0.2 cm,'' not ``0.1 $\times $ 0.2 cm$^{2}$.'' The abbreviation for ``seconds'' is ``s,'' not ``sec.'' Use ``Wb/m$^{2}$'' or ``webers per square meter,'' not ``webers/m$^{2}$.'' When expressing a range of values, write ``7 to 9'' or ``7--9,'' not ``7$\sim $9.'' A parenthetical statement at the end of a sentence is punctuated outside of the closing parenthesis (like this).}}}}
elements.sent-2411-034: If your native language is not English, please get a native English-speaking colleague to carefully proofread your paper.
elements.sent-2411-035: Try not to use too many typefaces in the same article.
elements.sent-2411-036: You're writing scholarly papers, not ransom notes.
elements.sent-2411-037: Also please remember that MathJax can't handle really weird typefaces.
elements.sent-2411-038: Number equations consecutively with equation numbers in parentheses flush with the right margin, as in \eqref{eq .}
elements.sent-2411-039: To make your equations more compact, you may use the solidus (~/~), the exp function, or appropriate exponents.
elements.sent-2411-040: Use parentheses to avoid ambiguities in denominators.
elements.sent-2411-041: Punctuate equations when they are part of a sentence, as in \begin{equation}E=mc^2.\label{eq}\end{equation} Be sure that the symbols in your equation have been defined before the equation appears or immediately following.
elements.sent-2411-042: Italicize symbols ($T$ might refer to temperature, but T is the unit tesla).
elements.sent-2411-043: Refer to ``\eqref{eq ,'' not ``Eq. \eqref{eq '' or ``equation \eqref{eq ,'' except at the beginning of a sentence: ``Equation \eqref{eq is $\ldots$ .''.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-044: Please use ``soft'' (e.g., \verb|\eqref{Eq |) cross references instead of ``hard'' references (e.g., \verb|(1)|).}
elements.sent-2411-045: That will make it possible to combine sections, add equations, or change the order of figures or citations without having to go through the file line by line.
elements.sent-2411-046: Please don't use the \verb|{eqnarray | equation environment.}
elements.sent-2411-048: The \verb|{eqnarray | environment leaves unsightly spaces around relation symbols.}
elements.sent-2411-049: Please note that the \verb|{subequations | environment in {\LaTeX will increment the main equation counter even when there are no equation numbers displayed.}}
elements.sent-2411-050: If you forget that, you might write an article in which the equation numbers skip from (17) to (20), causing the copy editors to wonder if you've discovered a new method of counting.
elements.sent-2411-051: It doesn't get the bibliographic data from thin air but from .bib files.
elements.sent-2411-052: If you use {\BibTeX to produce a bibliography you must send the .bib files.}
elements.sent-2411-053: If you assign the same label to a subsubsection and a table, you might find that Table I has been cross referenced as Table IV-B3.
elements.sent-2411-054: If you put a \verb|\label| command before the command that updates the counter it's supposed to be using, the label will pick up the last counter to be cross referenced instead.
elements.sent-2411-055: In particular, a \verb|\label| command should not go before the caption of a figure or a table.
elements.sent-2411-056: Do not use \verb|\nonumber| inside the \verb|{array | environment.}
elements.sent-2411-057: It will not stop equation numbers inside \verb|{array | (there won't be any anyway) and it might stop a wanted equation number in the surrounding equation.}
elements.sent-2411-058: If you are submitting your paper to a colorized journal, you can use the following two lines at the start of the article to ensure its appearance resembles the final copy: \begin{small \begin{tabular {l \verb+\+documentclass[journal,twoside,web]\{ieeecolor\} \verb+\+usepackage\{Journal\_Name\} \end{tabular \end{small.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-059: Use either SI (MKS) or CGS as primary units.
elements.sent-2411-060: This applies to papers in data storage.
elements.sent-2411-061: For example, write ``15 Gb/cm$^{2}$ (100 Gb/in$^{2 )$.'' An exception is when English units are used as identifiers in trade, such as ``3\textonehalf-in disk drive.'' Avoid combining SI and CGS units, such as current in amperes and magnetic field in oersteds.}
elements.sent-2411-062: This often leads to confusion because equations do not balance dimensionally.
elements.sent-2411-063: If you must use mixed units, clearly state the units for each quantity in an equation.
elements.sent-2411-064: The SI unit for magnetic field strength $H$ is A/m.
elements.sent-2411-065: However, if you wish to use units of T, either refer to magnetic flux density $B$ or magnetic field strength symbolized as $\mu _{0 H$.}
elements.sent-2411-066: Use the center dot to separate compound units, e.g., ``A$\cdot $m$^{2}$.''.
elements.sent-2411-067: The word ``data'' is plural, not singular.
elements.sent-2411-068: The subscript for the permeability of vacuum $\mu _{0}$ is zero, not a lowercase letter ``o.'' The term for residual magnetization is ``remanence''; the adjective is ``remanent''; do not write ``remnance'' or ``remnant.'' Use the word ``micrometer'' instead of ``micron.'' A graph within a graph is an ``inset,'' not an ``insert.'' The word ``alternatively'' is preferred to the word ``alternately'' (unless you really mean something that alternates).
elements.sent-2411-069: Use the word ``whereas'' instead of ``while'' (unless you are referring to simultaneous events).
elements.sent-2411-070: Do not use the word ``essentially'' to mean ``approximately'' or ``effectively.'' Do not use the word ``issue'' as a euphemism for ``problem.'' When compositions are not specified, separate chemical symbols by en-dashes; for example, ``NiMn'' indicates the intermetallic compound Ni$_{0.5}$Mn$_{0.5}$ whereas ``Ni--Mn'' indicates an alloy of some composition Ni$_{x}$Mn$_{1-x}$.
elements.figure-2411-001: \centerline{\includegraphics[width=\columnwidth]{Figures/Fig1.png}} \caption{Magnetization as a function of applied field. It is good practice to explain the significance of the figure in the caption.} \label{fig1}
elements.sent-2411-071: Be aware of the different meanings of the homophones ``affect'' (usually a verb) and ``effect'' (usually a noun), ``complement'' and ``compliment,'' ``discreet'' and ``discrete,'' ``principal'' (e.g., ``principal investigator'') and ``principle'' (e.g., ``principle of measurement'').
elements.sent-2411-072: Do not confuse ``imply'' and ``infer.'' Prefixes such as ``non,'' ``sub,'' ``micro,'' ``multi,'' and ``ultra'' are not independent words; they should be joined to the words they modify, usually without a hyphen.
elements.sent-2411-073: There is no period after the ``et'' in the Latin abbreviation ``et al.'' (it is also italicized).
elements.sent-2411-074: The abbreviation ``i.e.,'' means ``that is,'' and the abbreviation ``e.g.,'' means ``for example'' (these abbreviations are not italicized).
elements.sent-2411-075: A general IEEE styleguide is available at http://www.ieee.org/authortools.
elements.section-2411-004: Guidelines for Graphics Preparation and Submission
elements.sent-2411-076: The following list outlines the different types of graphics published in IEEE journals.
elements.sent-2411-077: They are categorized based on their construction, and use of color/shades of gray:.
elements.sent-2411-078: Such figures may include photographs, illustrations, multicolor graphs, and flowcharts.
elements.sent-2411-079: These figures should have no shades or half-tones of gray, only black and white.
elements.figure-2411-002: \caption{Units for Magnetic Properties} \label{table} \setlength{\tabcolsep}{3pt} \begin{tabular}{|p{25pt}|p{75pt}|p{115pt}|} Symbol Quantity Conversion from Gaussian and \par CGS EMU to SI $^{\mathrm{a}}$ $\Phi $ magnetic flux 1 Mx $\to 10^{-8}$ Wb $= 10^{-8}$ V$\cdot $s $B$ magnetic flux density, \par magnetic induction 1 G $\to 10^{-4}$ T $= 10^{-4}$ Wb/m$^{2}$ $H$ magnetic field strength 1 Oe $\to 10^{3}/(4\pi )$ A/m $m$ magnetic moment 1 erg/G $=$ 1 emu \par $\to 10^{-3}$ A$\cdot $m$^{2} = 10^{-3}$ J/T $M$ magnetization 1 erg/(G$\cdot $cm$^{3}) =$ 1 emu/cm$^{3}$ \par $\to 10^{3}$ A/m 4$\pi M$ magnetization 1 G $\to 10^{3}/(4\pi )$ A/m $\sigma $ specific magnetization 1 erg/(G$\cdot $g) $=$ 1 emu/g $\to $ 1 A$\cdot $m$^{2}$/kg $j$ magnetic dipole \par moment 1 erg/G $=$ 1 emu \par $\to 4\pi \times 10^{-10}$ Wb$\cdot $m $J$ magnetic polarization 1 erg/(G$\cdot $cm$^{3}) =$ 1 emu/cm$^{3}$ \par $\to 4\pi \times 10^{-4}$ T $\chi , \kappa $ susceptibility 1 $\to 4\pi $ $\chi_{\rho }$ mass susceptibility 1 cm$^{3}$/g $\to 4\pi \times 10^{-3}$ m$^{3}$/kg $\mu $ permeability 1 $\to 4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ H/m \par $= 4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ Wb/(A$\cdot $m) $\mu_{r}$ relative permeability $\mu \to \mu_{r}$ $w, W$ energy density 1 erg/cm$^{3} \to 10^{-1}$ J/m$^{3}$ $N, D$ demagnetizing factor 1 $\to 1/(4\pi )$ \multicolumn{3}{p{251pt}}{Vertical lines are optional in tables. Statements that serve as captions for the entire table do not need footnote letters. } \multicolumn{3}{p{251pt}}{$^{\mathrm{a}}$Gaussian units are the same as cg emu for magnetostatics; Mx $=$ maxwell, G $=$ gauss, Oe $=$ oersted; Wb $=$ weber, V $=$ volt, s $=$ second, T $=$ tesla, m $=$ meter, A $=$ ampere, J $=$ joule, kg $=$ kilogram, H $=$ henry.} \end{tabular} \label{tab1}
elements.sent-2411-080: Figures compiled of more than one sub-figure presented side-by-side, or stacked.
elements.sent-2411-081: If a multipart figure is made up of multiple figure types (one part is lineart, and another is grayscale or color) the figure should meet the stricter guidelines.
elements.sent-2411-082: Format and save your graphics using a suitable graphics processing program that will allow you to create the images as PostScript (PS), Encapsulated PostScript (.EPS), Tagged Image File Format (.TIFF), Portable Document Format (.PDF), Portable Network Graphics (.PNG), or Metapost (.MPS), sizes them, and adjusts the resolution settings.
elements.sent-2411-083: When submitting your final paper, your graphics should all be submitted individually in one of these formats along with the manuscript.
elements.sent-2411-084: Most charts, graphs, and tables are one column wide (3.5 inches/88 millimeters/21 picas) or page wide (7.16 inches/181 millimeters/43 picas).
elements.sent-2411-085: The maximum depth a graphic can be is 8.5 inches (216 millimeters/54 picas).
elements.sent-2411-086: When choosing the depth of a graphic, please allow space for a caption.
elements.sent-2411-087: Figures can be sized between column and page widths if the author chooses, however it is recommended that figures are not sized less than column width unless when necessary.
elements.sent-2411-088: There is currently one publication with column measurements that do not coincide with those listed above.
elements.sent-2411-089: Proceedings of the IEEE has a column measurement of 3.25 inches (82.5 millimeters/19.5 picas).
elements.sent-2411-090: The final printed size of author photographs is exactly 1 inch wide by 1.25 inches tall (25.4 millimeters$\,\times\,$31.75 millimeters/6 picas$\,\times\,$7.5 picas).
elements.sent-2411-091: Author photos printed in editorials measure 1.59 inches wide by 2 inches tall (40 millimeters$\,\times\,$50 millimeters/9.5 picas$\,\times\,$12 picas).
elements.sent-2411-092: The proper resolution of your figures will depend on the type of figure it is as defined in the ``Types of Figures'' section.
elements.sent-2411-093: Author photographs, color, and grayscale figures should be at least 300dpi.
elements.sent-2411-094: Line art, including tables should be a minimum of 600dpi.
elements.sent-2411-095: In order to preserve the figures' integrity across multiple computer platforms, we accept files in the following formats: .EPS/.PDF/.PS.
elements.sent-2411-096: All fonts must be embedded or text converted to outlines in order to achieve the best-quality results.
elements.sent-2411-097: The term color space refers to the entire sum of colors that can be represented within the said medium.
elements.sent-2411-098: For our purposes, the three main color spaces are Grayscale, RGB (red/green/blue) and CMYK (cyan/magenta/yellow/black).
elements.sent-2411-099: RGB is generally used with on-screen graphics, whereas CMYK is used for printing purposes.
elements.sent-2411-100: All color figures should be generated in RGB or CMYK color space.
elements.sent-2411-101: Grayscale images should be submitted in Grayscale color space.
elements.sent-2411-102: Line art may be provided in grayscale OR bitmap colorspace.
elements.sent-2411-103: Note that ``bitmap colorspace'' and ``bitmap file format'' are not the same thing.
elements.sent-2411-104: When bitmap color space is selected, .TIF/.TIFF/.PNG are the recommended file formats.
elements.sent-2411-105: When preparing your graphics IEEE suggests that you use of one of the following Open Type fonts: Times New Roman, Helvetica, Arial, Cambria, and Symbol.
elements.sent-2411-106: If you are supplying EPS, PS, or PDF files all fonts must be embedded.
elements.sent-2411-107: Some fonts may only be native to your operating system; without the fonts embedded, parts of the graphic may be distorted or missing.
elements.sent-2411-108: A safe option when finalizing your figures is to strip out the fonts before you save the files, creating ``outline'' type.
elements.sent-2411-109: This converts fonts to artwork what will appear uniformly on any screen.
elements.sent-2411-110: Figure axis labels are often a source of confusion.
elements.sent-2411-112: As an example, write the quantity ``Magnetization,'' or ``Magnetization M,'' not just ``M.'' Put units in parentheses.
elements.sent-2411-113: Do not label axes only with units.
elements.sent-2411-114: As in Fig. 1, for example, write ``Magnetization (A/m)'' or ``Magnetization (A$\cdot$m$^{-1}$),'' not just ``A/m.'' Do not label axes with a ratio of quantities and units.
elements.sent-2411-115: For example, write ``Temperature (K),'' not ``Temperature/K.'' Multipliers can be especially confusing.
elements.sent-2411-116: Write ``Magnetization (kA/m)'' or ``Magnetization (10$^{3}$ A/m).'' Do not write ``Magnetization (A/m)$\,\times\,$1000'' because the reader would not know whether the top axis label in Fig. 1 meant 16000 A/m or 0.016 A/m.
elements.sent-2411-117: Figure labels should be legible, approximately 8 to 10 point type.
elements.subsubsection-2411-006: Subfigure Labels in Multipart Figures and Tables
elements.sent-2411-118: Multipart figures should be combined and labeled before final submission.
elements.sent-2411-119: Labels should appear centered below each subfigure in 8 point Times New Roman font in the format of (a) (b) (c).
elements.sent-2411-120: Figures (line artwork or photographs) should be named starting with the first 5 letters of the author's last name.
elements.sent-2411-121: The next characters in the filename should be the number that represents the sequential location of this image in your article.
elements.sent-2411-122: For example, in author ``Anderson's'' paper, the first three figures would be named ander1.tif, ander2.tif, and ander3.ps.
elements.sent-2411-123: Tables should contain only the body of the table (not the caption) and should be named similarly to figures, except that `.t' is inserted in-between the author's name and the table number.
elements.sent-2411-124: For example, author Anderson's first three tables would be named ander.t1.tif, ander.t2.ps, ander.t3.eps.
elements.sent-2411-125: Author photographs should be named using the first five characters of the pictured author's last name.
elements.sent-2411-126: For example, four author photographs for a paper may be named: oppen.ps, moshc.tif, chen.eps, and duran.pdf.
elements.sent-2411-127: If two authors or more have the same last name, their first initial(s) can be substituted for the fifth, fourth, third$\ldots$ letters of their surname until the degree where there is differentiation.
elements.sent-2411-128: For example, two authors Michael and Monica Oppenheimer's photos would be named oppmi.tif, and oppmo.eps.
elements.subsection-2411-015: Referencing a Figure or Table Within Your Paper
elements.sent-2411-129: When referencing your figures and tables within your paper, use the abbreviation ``Fig.'' even at the beginning of a sentence.
elements.sent-2411-130: Do not abbreviate ``Table.'' Tables should be numbered with Roman Numerals.
elements.subsection-2411-016: Checking Your Figures: The IEEE Graphics Analyzer
elements.sent-2411-131: The IEEE Graphics Analyzer enables authors to pre-screen their graphics for compliance with IEEE Transactions and Journals standards before submission.
elements.sent-2411-132: The online tool, located at http://graphicsqc.ieee.org/, allows authors to upload their graphics in order to check that each file is the correct file format, resolution, size and colorspace; that no fonts are missing or corrupt; that figures are not compiled in layers or have transparency, and that they are named according to the IEEE Transactions and Journals naming convention.
elements.sent-2411-133: At the end of this automated process, authors are provided with a detailed report on each graphic within the web applet, as well as by email.
elements.sent-2411-134: For more information on using the Graphics Analyzer or any other graphics related topic, contact the IEEE Graphics Help Desk by e-mail at graphics@ieee.org.
elements.sent-2411-135: Because IEEE will do the final formatting of your paper, you do not need to position figures and tables at the top and bottom of each column.
elements.sent-2411-136: In fact, all figures, figure captions, and tables can be placed at the end of your paper.
elements.sent-2411-137: In addition to, or even in lieu of submitting figures within your final manuscript, figures should be submitted individually, separate from the manuscript in one of the file formats listed above in Section \ref{formats .}
elements.sent-2411-138: Place figure captions below the figures; place table titles above the tables.
elements.sent-2411-139: Please do not include captions as part of the figures, or put them in ``text boxes'' linked to the figures.
elements.sent-2411-140: Also, do not place borders around the outside of your figures.
elements.sent-2411-141: All IEEE Transactions, Journals, and Letters allow an author to publish color figures on IEEE Xplore\textregistered\ at no charge, and automatically convert them to grayscale for print versions.
elements.sent-2411-142: In most journals, figures and tables may alternatively be printed in color if an author chooses to do so.
elements.sent-2411-143: Please note that this service comes at an extra expense to the author.
elements.sent-2411-144: If you intend to have print color graphics, include a note with your final paper indicating which figures or tables you would like to be handled that way, and stating that you are willing to pay the additional fee.
elements.sent-2411-145: A conclusion section is not required.
elements.sent-2411-146: Although a conclusion may review the main points of the paper, do not replicate the abstract as the conclusion.
elements.sent-2411-147: A conclusion might elaborate on the importance of the work or suggest applications and extensions.
elements.sent-2411-148: The preferred spelling of the word ``acknowledgment'' in American English is without an ``e'' after the ``g.'' Use the singular heading even if you have many acknowledgments.
elements.sent-2411-149: Avoid expressions such as ``One of us (S.B.A.) would like to thank $\ldots$ .'' Instead, write ``F.. A.
elements.sent-2411-150: Author thanks $\ldots$ .'' In most cases, sponsor and financial support acknowledgments are placed in the unnumbered footnote on the first page, not here.
elements.sent-2411-151: When they are, they appear on the line, in square brackets, inside the punctuation.
elements.sent-2411-152: Multiple references are each numbered with separate brackets.
elements.sent-2411-153: When citing a section in a book, please give the relevant page numbers.
elements.sent-2411-154: In text, refer simply to the reference number.
elements.sent-2411-155: Do not use ``Ref.'' or ``reference'' except at the beginning of a sentence: ``Reference \cite{b3 shows $\ldots$ .'' Please do not use automatic endnotes in Word, rather, type the reference list at the end of the paper using the ``References'' style.}
elements.sent-2411-156: Reference numbers are set flush left and form a column of their own, hanging out beyond the body of the reference.
elements.sent-2411-157: The reference numbers are on the line, enclosed in square brackets.
elements.sent-2411-158: In all references, the given name of the author or editor is abbreviated to the initial only and precedes the last name.
elements.sent-2411-159: Use them all; use et al. only if names are not given.
elements.sent-2411-160: Use commas around Jr., Sr., and III in names.. Abbreviate conference titles.
elements.sent-2411-161: When citing IEEE transactions, provide the issue number, page range, volume number, year, and/or month if available.
elements.sent-2411-162: When referencing a patent, provide the day and the month of issue, or application.
elements.sent-2411-163: References may not include all information; please obtain and include relevant information.
elements.sent-2411-165: There must be only one reference with each number.
elements.sent-2411-166: If there is a URL included with the print reference, it can be included at the end of the reference.
elements.sent-2411-167: Other than books, capitalize only the first word in a paper title, except for proper nouns and element symbols.
elements.sent-2411-168: For papers published in translation journals, please give the English citation first, followed by the original foreign-language citation See the end of this document for formats and examples of common references.
elements.sent-2411-169: For a complete discussion of references and their formats, see the IEEE style manual at http://www.ieee.org/authortools.
elements.sent-2411-170: Number footnotes separately in superscript numbers.\footnote{It is recommended that footnotes be avoided (except for the unnumbered footnote with the receipt date on the first page).}
elements.sent-2411-171: Instead, try to integrate the footnote information into the text.
elements.sent-2411-172: Place the actual footnote at the bottom of the column in which it is cited; do not put footnotes in the reference list (endnotes).
elements.sent-2411-173: Use letters for table footnotes (see Table \ref{table ).}
elements.sent-2411-174: When you submit your final version (after your paper has been accepted), print it in two-column format, including figures and tables.
elements.sent-2411-175: You must also send your final manuscript on a disk, via e-mail, or through a Web manuscript submission system as directed by the society contact.
elements.sent-2411-176: You may use Zip for large files, or compress files using Compress, Pkzip, Stuffit, or Gzip.
elements.sent-2411-177: Also, send a sheet of paper or PDF with complete contact information for all authors.
elements.sent-2411-178: Include full mailing addresses, telephone numbers, fax numbers, and e-mail addresses.
elements.sent-2411-179: This information will be used to send each author a complimentary copy of the journal in which the paper appears.
elements.sent-2411-180: In addition, designate one author as the ``corresponding author.'' This is the author to whom proofs of the paper will be sent.
elements.sent-2411-181: Proofs are sent to the corresponding author only.
elements.sent-2411-182: Contributions to the Transactions, Journals, and Letters may be submitted electronically on IEEE's on-line manuscript submission and peer-review system, ScholarOne\textregistered\ Manuscripts.
elements.sent-2411-183: You can get a listing of the publications that participate in ScholarOne at http://www.ieee.org/publications\_standards/publications/\discretionary{ { { authors/authors\_submission.html.}}}
elements.sent-2411-184: First check if you have an existing account.
elements.sent-2411-185: If there is none, please create a new account.
elements.sent-2411-186: After logging in, go to your Author Center and click ``Submit First Draft of a New Manuscript.'' Along with other information, you will be asked to select the subject from a pull-down list.
elements.sent-2411-187: Depending on the journal, there are various steps to the submission process; you must complete all steps for a complete submission.
elements.sent-2411-188: At the end of each step you must click ``Save and Continue''; just uploading the paper is not sufficient.
elements.sent-2411-189: After the last step, you should see a confirmation that the submission is complete.
elements.sent-2411-190: You should also receive an e-mail confirmation.
elements.sent-2411-191: For inquiries regarding the submission of your paper on ScholarOne Manuscripts, please contact oprs-support@ieee.org or call +1 732 465 5861.
elements.sent-2411-192: ScholarOne Manuscripts will accept files for review in various formats.
elements.sent-2411-193: Please check the guidelines of the specific journal for which you plan to submit.
elements.sent-2411-194: You will be asked to file an electronic copyright form immediately upon completing the submission process (authors are responsible for obtaining any security clearances).
elements.sent-2411-195: Failure to submit the electronic copyright could result in publishing delays later.
elements.sent-2411-196: You will also have the opportunity to designate your article as ``open access'' if you agree to pay the IEEE open access fee.
elements.sent-2411-197: Upon acceptance, you will receive an email with specific instructions regarding the submission of your final files.
elements.sent-2411-198: To avoid any delays in publication, please be sure to follow these instructions.
elements.sent-2411-199: Most journals require that final submissions be uploaded through ScholarOne Manuscripts, although some may still accept final submissions via email.
elements.sent-2411-200: Final submissions should include source files of your accepted manuscript, high quality graphic files, and a formatted pdf file.
elements.sent-2411-201: If you have any questions regarding the final submission process, please contact the administrative contact for the journal.
elements.sent-2411-202: In addition to this, upload a file with complete contact information for all authors.
elements.sent-2411-203: Designate the author who submitted the manuscript on ScholarOne Manuscripts as the ``corresponding author.'' This is the only author to whom proofs of the paper will be sent.
elements.sent-2411-204: Authors must submit an electronic IEEE Copyright Form (eCF) upon submitting their final manuscript files.
elements.sent-2411-205: You can access the eCF system through your manuscript submission system or through the Author Gateway.
elements.sent-2411-206: You are responsible for obtaining any necessary approvals and/or security clearances.
elements.sent-2411-207: For additional information on intellectual property rights, visit the IEEE Intellectual Property Rights department web page at http://www.ieee.org/publications\_standards/publications/rights/\discretionary{ { { index.html.}}}
elements.sent-2411-208: The general IEEE policy requires that authors should only submit original work that has neither appeared elsewhere for publication, nor is under review for another refereed publication.
elements.sent-2411-209: The submitting author must disclose all prior publication(s) and current submissions when submitting a manuscript.
elements.sent-2411-210: Do not publish ``preliminary'' data or results.
elements.sent-2411-211: The submitting author is responsible for obtaining agreement of all coauthors and any consent required from employers or sponsors before submitting an article.
elements.sent-2411-212: The IEEE Transactions and Journals Department strongly discourages courtesy authorship; it is the obligation of the authors to cite only relevant prior work.
elements.sent-2411-213: The IEEE Transactions and Journals Department does not publish conference records or proceedings, but can publish articles related to conferences that have undergone rigorous peer review.
elements.sent-2411-214: Minimally, two reviews are required for every article submitted for peer review.
elements.sent-2411-215: The two types of contents of that are published are; 1) peer-reviewed and 2) archival.
elements.sent-2411-216: The Transactions and Journals Department publishes scholarly articles of archival value as well as tutorial expositions and critical reviews of classical subjects and topics of current interest.
elements.sent-2411-217: Authors should consider the following points:.
elements.sent-2411-218: Technical papers submitted for publication must advance the state of knowledge and must cite relevant prior work.
elements.sent-2411-219: The length of a submitted paper should be commensurate with the importance, or appropriate to the complexity, of the work.
elements.sent-2411-220: For example, an obvious extension of previously published work might not be appropriate for publication or might be adequately treated in just a few pages.
elements.sent-2411-221: Authors must convince both peer reviewers and the editors of the scientific and technical merit of a paper; the standards of proof are higher when extraordinary or unexpected results are reported.
elements.sent-2411-222: Because replication is required for scientific progress, papers submitted for publication must provide sufficient information to allow readers to perform similar experiments or calculations and use the reported results.
elements.sent-2411-223: Although not everything need be disclosed, a paper must contain new, useable, and fully described information.
elements.sent-2411-224: For example, a specimen's chemical composition need not be reported if the main purpose of a paper is to introduce a new measurement technique.
elements.sent-2411-225: Authors should expect to be challenged by reviewers if the results are not supported by adequate data and critical details.
elements.sent-2411-226: Papers that describe ongoing work or announce the latest technical achievement, which are suitable for presentation at a professional conference, may not be appropriate for publication.
elements.sent-2411-227: Basic format for books: J.. K.
elements.sent-2411-228: Author, ``Title of chapter in the book,'' in Title of His Published Book, xth ed.
elements.sent-2411-229: City of Publisher, (only U.S.. State), Country: Abbrev.
elements.sent-2411-230: Basic format for periodicals: J.. K.
elements.sent-2411-232: Title of Periodical, Vol. x, No. $x, $pp. xxx--xxx, Abbrev.. Month, year, DOI.
elements.sent-2411-234: Basic format for reports: J.. K.
elements.sent-2411-236: Name of Co., City of Co., Abbrev.. State, Country, Rep.
elements.sent-2411-237: Basic format for handbooks: Name of Manual/Handbook, x ed., Abbrev.
elements.sent-2411-238: Name of Co., City of Co., Abbrev.
elements.sent-2411-239: State, Country, year, pp. xxx--xxx.. See \cite{b8,b9 .}
elements.sent-2411-240: Basic format for books (when available online): J.. K.
elements.sent-2411-241: Author, ``Title of chapter in the book,'' in Title of Published Book, $x$th ed.
elements.sent-2411-242: City of Publisher, State, Country: Abbrev.
elements.sent-2411-244: Basic format for journals (when available online): J.. K.
elements.sent-2411-245: Title of Periodical, Vol. $x$, No. $x$, pp. xxx--xxx, Abbrev.. Month, year.
elements.sent-2411-246: Accessed on: Month, Day, year, DOI: 10.1109.XXX.123456, [Online].
elements.sent-2411-248: Basic format for papers presented at conferences (when available online): J.K.. Author.
elements.sent-2411-250: Basic format for reports and handbooks (when available online): J.. K.
elements.sent-2411-252: Basic format for computer programs and electronic documents (when available online): Legislative body.
elements.sent-2411-253: Number of Congress, Session.. (year, month day).
elements.sent-2411-254: Number of bill or resolution, Title.. [Type of medium].
elements.sent-2411-255: Available: site/path/file NOTE: ISO recommends that capitalization follow the accepted practice for the language or script in which the information is given.. See \cite{b20 .}
elements.sent-2411-256: Basic format for patents (when available online): Name of the invention, by inventor's name.. (year, month day).
elements.sent-2411-259: Basic formatfor conference proceedings (published): J.. K.
elements.sent-2411-260: Author, ``Title of paper,'' in Abbreviated Name of Conf., City of Conf., Abbrev.
elements.sent-2411-261: State (if given), Country, year, pp. xxxxxx.. See \cite{b22 .}
elements.sent-2411-262: Example for papers presented at conferences (unpublished): See \cite{b23 .}
elements.sent-2411-263: Basic format for patents$:$ J.. K.
elements.sent-2411-265: Patent x xxx xxx, Abbrev.. Month, day, year.
elements.sent-2411-267: Basic format for theses (M.S.) and dissertations (Ph.D.): \begin{enumerate.}
elements.sent-2411-268: Author, ``Title of thesis,'' M.S.. thesis, Abbrev.
elements.sent-2411-269: Univ., City of Univ., Abbrev.. State, year.
elements.sent-2411-270: Author, ``Title of dissertation,'' Ph.D.. dissertation, Abbrev.
elements.sent-2411-271: Basic format for the most common types of unpublished references: \begin{enumerate.}
elements.sent-2411-272: Author, private communication, Abbrev.. Month, year.
elements.sent-2411-274: Author, ``Title of paper,'' to be published.
elements.sent-2411-276: Title of Standard, Standard number, date.
elements.sent-2411-277: Title of Standard, Standard number, Corporate author, location, date.
elements.sent-2411-279: Example when using et al.: See \cite{b34 .}
elements.sent-2411-280: Author (M'76--SM'81--F'87) and all authors may include biographies.
elements.sent-2411-281: Biographies are often not included in conference-related papers.
elements.sent-2411-282: This author became a Member (M) of IEEE in 1976, a Senior Member (SM) in 1981, and a Fellow (F) in 1987.
elements.sent-2411-283: The first paragraph may contain a place and/or date of birth (list place, then date).
elements.sent-2411-284: Next, the author's educational background is listed.
elements.sent-2411-285: The degrees should be listed with type of degree in what field, which institution, city, state, and country, and year the degree was earned.
elements.sent-2411-286: The author's major field of study should be lower-cased.
elements.sent-2411-287: The second paragraph uses the pronoun of the person (he or she) and not the author's last name.
elements.sent-2411-288: It lists military and work experience, including summer and fellowship jobs.
elements.sent-2411-290: The current job must have a location; previous positions may be listed without one.
elements.sent-2411-291: Information concerning previous publications may be included.
elements.sent-2411-292: Try not to list more than three books or published articles.
elements.sent-2411-293: The format for listing publishers of a book within the biography is: title of book (publisher name, year) similar to a reference.
elements.sent-2411-294: Current and previous research interests end the paragraph.
elements.sent-2411-295: The third paragraph begins with the author's title and last name (e.g., Dr.\ Smith, Prof.\ Jones, Mr.\ Kajor, Ms.\ Hunter).
elements.sent-2411-296: List any memberships in professional societies other than the IEEE.
elements.sent-2411-297: Finally, list any awards and work for IEEE committees and publications.
elements.sent-2411-298: If a photograph is provided, it should be of good quality, and professional-looking.
elements.sent-2411-299: Following are two examples of an author's biography.
elements.sent-2411-300: Author was born in Greenwich Village, New York, NY, USA in 1977.
elements.sent-2411-301: He received the B.S.. and M.S.
elements.sent-2411-302: From 2001 to 2004, he was a Research Assistant with the Princeton Plasma Physics Laboratory.
elements.sent-2411-303: Since 2009, he has been an Assistant Professor with the Mechanical Engineering Department, Texas A{\& M University, College Station.}
elements.sent-2411-304: He is the author of three books, more than 150 articles, and more than 70 inventions.
elements.sent-2411-305: His research interests include high-pressure and high-density nonthermal plasma discharge processes and applications, microscale plasma discharges, discharges in liquids, spectroscopic diagnostics, plasma propulsion, and innovation plasma applications.
elements.sent-2411-306: He is an Associate Editor of the journal Earth, Moon, Planets, and holds two patents.
elements.sent-2411-307: Dr. Author was a recipient of the International Association of Geomagnetism and Aeronomy Young Scientist Award for Excellence in 2008, and the IEEE Electromagnetic Compatibility Society Best Symposium Paper Award in 2011.
elements.sent-2411-308: He is currently pursuing the Ph.D.
elements.sent-2411-309: From 2008 to 2009, he was a Research Assistant with the Institute of Physics, Academia Sinica, Tapei, Taiwan.
elements.sent-2411-310: His research interest includes the development of surface processing and biological/medical treatment techniques using nonthermal atmospheric pressure plasmas, fundamental study of plasma sources, and fabrication of micro- or nanostructured surfaces.
elements.sent-2411-311: Mr. Author's awards and honors include the Frew Fellowship (Australian Academy of Science), the I.. I.
elements.sent-2411-312: Rabi Prize (APS), the European Frequency and Time Forum Award, the Carl Zeiss Research Award, the William F.
elements.sent-2411-313: Meggers Award and the Adolph Lomb Medal (OSA).. \end{IEEEbiography.}
elements.sent-2411-314: Date of publication \textcolor{red {add date ; date of current version \textcolor{red {add date .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-315: This work was supported by the Defense Advanced Research Project Agency Microsystems Technology Office (DARPA MTO) COFFEE TA1 project under Contract HR001122C0088 and developed in collaboration with RTX Missiles and Defense and Naval Research Lab (NRL).
elements.sent-2411-316: The review of this letter was arranged by Editor \textcolor{red {add name .. (Corresponding authors: L.}}
elements.sent-2411-318: This letter reports on Solidly-Mounted Bidimensional Mode Resonators (S2MRs) based on 30\% Scandium-doped Aluminum Nitride (ScAlN) on Silicon Carbide (SiC), operating near 16 GHz.
elements.sent-2411-319: Experimental results demonstrate mechanical quality factors ($Q_m$) as high as 380, electromechanical coupling coefficients ($k_t^2$) of 4.5\%, an overall Figure of Merit ($FOM = Q_m \cdot k_t^2$) exceeding 17, and power handling greater than 20 dBm for devices closely matched to 50~$\Omega$.
elements.sent-2411-320: To the best of the authors' knowledge, S2MRs exhibit the highest Key Performance Indicators (KPIs) among solidly mounted resonators in the K$_u$ band, paving the way for the integration of nanoacoustic devices on fast substrates with high-power electronics, tailored for military and harsh-environment applications.
elements.sent-2411-321: Emerging communication paradigms are driving the demand for low Size, Weight, and Power (SWaP) Radio Frequency (RF) electronic components capable of performing signal processing, timing, and impedance matching in the K$_u$ band and beyond \cite{dunworth_28ghz_2018}\cite{zhong_-chip_2017 .}
elements.sent-2411-322: Specifically, in the context of electromagnetic (EM) spectrum filtering, advanced 5G and proposed 6G commercial applications stand to benefit significantly from the development of compact, high-performance, and mass-manufacturable on-chip filters designed for handheld and wearable devices \cite{aigner_baw_2018}\cite{matthaiou_road_2021 .}
elements.sent-2411-323: Similarly, Satellite Communications (SATCOM) and military applications, such as Active Electronically Scanned Arrays (AESA) \cite{gultepe_1024-element_2021 , would greatly benefit from technologies that enable monolithic integration, high power handling, and reliable operation at elevated temperatures (up to hundreds of degrees Celsius) \cite{alexandru_monolithic_2013}\cite{yuvaraja_wide_2023 .}}
elements.sent-2411-324: While traditional EM filters \cite{zheng_compact_2017}\cite{xu_synthesis_2016 and novel spintronic devices \cite{du_frequency_2024 remain bulky and challenging to scale, researchers have increasingly turned their attention to piezoelectric acoustic Micro- and Nanoelectromechanical Systems (M/NEMS) as a promising alternative for RF filtering at higher frequencies \cite{kochhar_x-band_2023 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-325: Acoustic MEMS devices, such as Surface Acoustic Wave (SAW) \cite{takai_ihp_2017}\cite{takai_high-performance_2019 and Film Bulk Acoustic Resonators (FBARs) \cite{ruby_decade_2011}\cite{hagelauer_microwave_2023 , represent the state-of-the-art solutions in the field, offering high selectivity and low insertion loss ($IL$) \cite{vetury_xbaw_2022 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-326: However, their performance beyond 5 GHz has been hindered by challenges such as declining quality factors ($Q$) and the need to operate within the nanoacoustic domain, where sound propagation speeds are significantly lower than those of EM waves, increasing fabrication complexity \cite{kramer_57_2022 .}
elements.sent-2411-327: Recent advances have demonstrated that acoustic NEMS devices can retain high $Q$ factors even in the mmWave band \cite{giribaldi_up-scaling_2024 , making them viable candidates for integrated nanoacoustic filters with performance metrics compatible with modern communication systems stringent requirements \cite{giribaldi_compact_2024 .}}
elements.figure-2411-003: \centerline{\includegraphics[width=\columnwidth]{Figures/Fig1.png}} \caption{a) Cross-section of the proposed slow-on-fast solidly mounted bidimensional mode resonator (S2MR); b) Von Mises equivalent stress ($\sigma_{Mises}$ and $x-$ and $z-$ stress tensors ($\sigma_{xx}$ and $\sigma_{zz}$, respectively) according to COMSOL\textregistered~Finite Element Analysis (FEA) simulations; c) Displacement magnitude ($d$) and displacements in the $x-$ and $z-$ directions ($u$ and $w$, respectively) according to COMSOL\textregistered~FEA simulations.} \label{fig:enter-label}
elements.sent-2411-328: In this letter, nanoacoustic Solidly-Mounted Bidimensional Mode Resonators (S2MR) operating at approximately 16 GHz are presented.
elements.sent-2411-329: This class of devices leverages a slow-on-fast optimized Sezawa mode in a thin film of 30\%-doped Scandium Aluminum Nitride (ScAlN) on Silicon Carbide (SiC) \cite{du_near_2024 .}
elements.sent-2411-330: Key performance metrics include a mechanical quality factor ($Q_m$) of 380, an electromechanical coupling coefficient ($k_t^2$) greater than 4.5\%, and a resulting Figure of Merit (FoM) of 17.
elements.sent-2411-331: Additionally, S2MRs demonstrate power handling exceeding 20 dBm and offer a clear path for further performance enhancement.
elements.sent-2411-332: Their compatibility with monolithic integration on high-power electronic platforms such as SiC or diamond, combined with their robustness in harsh environments, positions these devices as a promising solution for high-performance filtering applications in both commercial and military domains.
elements.figure-2411-004: \centerline{\includegraphics[width=\columnwidth]{Figures/Fig2.png}} \caption{a) COMSOL\textregistered~FEA simulated electromechanical coupling ($k_t^2$) and propagation velocity at resonance ($v_{p_s}$) as a function of normalized piezoelectric film thickness ($h$) over acoustic wavelength ($\lambda$) for Scandium-doping concentrations ($Sc_{\%}$) ranging between 0 and 40\%. Aluminum (Al) top electrodes with a coverage ($c$) of 50\% and normalized metal thickness ($t_m$) over $\lambda$ of 0.125 are assumed. Higher Sc concentrations soften the material, hence increasing the coupling and reducing the propagation velocity; b) $k_t^2$ and $v_{p_s}$ as a function of $h/\lambda$ for normalized Al $t_m/\lambda$ ranging between 0.075 and 0.225, $Sc_{\%}$ of 30\%, and 50\% IDT coverage; c) Contour plot of $k_t^2$ as a function of $h/\lambda$ and $t_m/\lambda$ for 50\% coverage Al IDTs and 30\% Sc-doping. A unique local maximum for $h/\lambda$ = 0.65 and $t_m/\lambda$ = 0.125 exists. FEA simulated Von Mises stress distributions for $h/\lambda$ = 0.6 and $t_m/\lambda$ ranging between 0.025 and 0.325, highlighting the electrode-induced stress shift towards the piezoelectric-metal interface when thicker IDTs are adopted. } \label{fig:optmize}
elements.sent-2411-333: The embodiment of the proposed Scandium Aluminum Nitride (ScAlN) nanoacoustic Solidly-Mounted Bidimensional Resonators (S2MR) consists of a thin film ($h$) of piezoelectric material solidly-mounted on a fast, bulk substrate of Silicon Carbide (SiC) with patterned metallic Interdigitated Transducers (IDTs) spaced by half acoustic wavelength ($\lambda$) on the top surface (Fig.~\ref{fig:enter-label a).}
elements.sent-2411-334: From a physical perspective, S2MRs are slow, dispersive Surface Acoustic Wave (SAW) devices exploiting a highly-optimized Sezawa mode \cite{ahmed_super-high-frequency_2023}\cite{hadj-larbi_sezawa_2019}\cite{sezawa_dispersion_1927 on a substrate with high propagation velocity ($v_p$).}
elements.sent-2411-335: Similarly to Cross-Sectional Lamé Mode Resonators (CLMRs), they exploit both the $d_{33}$ and $d_{31}$ piezoelectric coefficient to generate a bidimensional acoustic standing wave with significant stress and displacement components in the lateral ($x$) and thickness ($z$) directions, as confirmed by COMSOL\textregistered~ Multiphysics Finite Element Analysis (FEA) simulations (Fig.~\ref{fig:enter-label b-c).}
elements.sent-2411-336: The large acoustic impedance mismatch between the active layer and the substrate is pivotal in constraining the acoustic energy into the piezoelectric medium, hence ensuring the existence of the resonant mode with large quality factor ($Q$) and electromechanical coupling ($k_t^2$).
elements.sent-2411-337: Metal-metal and metal-insulator layered Bragg reflectors \cite{barrera_18_2024 , sapphire \cite{guida_solidly_2023 , silicon carbide \cite{colombo_solidly_2024 , and diamond \cite{fujii_diamond_2020 substrates exhibit mechanical properties suitable for sustaining S2MRs in Sc-doped AlN.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-338: Finite element analysis (FEA) simulations based on the a~priori material properties of ScAlN, as described by Caro et al. \cite{caro_piezoelectric_2015 , reveal a super linear growth in $k_t^2$ at higher levels of Sc-doping ($Sc_{\%}$).}
elements.sent-2411-339: This behavior is attributed to the combined effects of increased piezoelectric coefficients and a reduction in propagation velocity (Fig.~\ref{fig:optmize a).}
elements.sent-2411-340: Despite the presence of a local $k_t^2$ maxima for an $h/\lambda$ of 0.6, this class of devices retains sub-optimal, large coupling over a broad range of $v_p$, thus enabling single-step, lithographic multi-frequency definition for the synthesis of on-chip acoustic filter banks \cite{giribaldi_compact_2024 .}
elements.sent-2411-341: Ultimately, S2MRs require tailored electrode material-thickness ($t_m$) optimization to maximize the coupling between the electric field laterally excited by the IDTs \cite{murata_improvement_2009 and the Sezawa's mode induced acoustic strain field.}
elements.sent-2411-342: As reported in Figs.~\ref{fig:optmize b-c, $h$, and $t_m$ must be co-optimized for a given IDT material, coverage ($c$), and $Sc_{\%}$, to ensure ideal energy localization in the piezoelectric medium.}
elements.sent-2411-343: In this work, 30\%-doped AlN-seeded ScAlN, 6H-SiC, and patterned aluminum (Al) with a 50\% metallization ratio ($c$) are implemented as the active layer, fast substrate, and IDT metal, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-344: While the material selection is primarily driven by established fabrication processes \cite{giribaldi_compact_2024 , the choice of the fast substrate hints at the potential for future monolithic integration of RF acoustic front-ends with high-power electronics, tailored for military and harsh-environment applications.}
elements.sent-2411-345: According to the FEA simulations reported in Fig.~\ref{fig:optmize b, $h$ is set to 250~nm, $t_m$ to 50~nm, and $\lambda$ to 400~nm to target a frequency of operation ($f_s$) of 16 GHz and maximized coupling.}
elements.sent-2411-346: The fabrication process adopted for the S2MRs manufacturing is illustrated in Fig. \ref{fig:device a.}
elements.sent-2411-347: The process begins with the in-house deposition of a 230 nm layer of 30\% ScAlN onto a 4-inch SiC substrate via reactive sputtering, using a commercial-grade Evatec CLUSTERLINE\textregistered~200 II system and a 12-inch casted target.
elements.sent-2411-348: The ScAlN thin film is sputtered onto a pre-deposited 20 nm-thick AlN seed layer \cite{hardy_nucleation_2023 , which serves as a templated nucleation layer to promote the growth of highly crystalline, $c$-axis-oriented piezoelectric films \cite{felice_energetics_1996 .}}
elements.figure-2411-005: \centerline{\includegraphics[width=\columnwidth]{Figures/Fig3.png}} \caption{a) Diagram of the micro-fabrication process adopted for the manufacturing of ScAlN S2MRs; b) SEM image of the sputtered 30\%-doped ScAlN thin film surface, reporting low Abnormally Oriented Grains (AOGs) density; c) X-Ray Diffraction (XRD) Full-Width-Half-Maximum (FWHM) diagram, highlighting excellent crystallinity; and d) SEM image of a fabricated S2MR operating around 16 GHz. The reported device has an electrode aperture ($L_e$) of 5.3 \si{\micro\metre}, and acoustic wavelength ($\lambda$) of 400 nm, and 120 finger pairs.} \label{fig:device}
elements.sent-2411-349: Characterization using Scanning Electron Microscope (SEM), Abnormally Oriented Grain (AOG) surface density ($AOG_{\%}$) and average dimension ($AOG_{avg}$) evaluation \cite{spagnuolo_image_2024 , as well as X-Ray Diffraction (XRD) Full Width Half Maximum (FWHM) measurements confirmed state-of-the-art crystallinity.}
elements.sent-2411-350: The results show an almost AOG-free top surface ($AOG_{\%}$ = 0.11\% and $AOG_{avg}$ = 108~nm) and a Rocking Curve (RC) of 1.5\textdegree (Figs.~\ref{fig:device b-c).}
elements.sent-2411-351: Subsequently, Interdigital Transducers (IDTs) are defined via e-beam lithography, necessitated by the critical feature size of 100 nm.
elements.sent-2411-352: A 50 nm thin film is deposited through thermal evaporation, followed by an overnight lift-off in 1165 Microposit remover.
elements.sent-2411-353: Finally, pads and interconnects are patterned using thermal evaporation of 250 nm of gold (Au), followed by a lift-off process.
elements.figure-2411-006: \centerline{\includegraphics[width=\columnwidth]{Figures/Fig4.png}} \caption{a) Admittance response ($Y_{12}$) and phase ($\theta$) of a fabricated S2MR operating around 16 GHz and exhibiting the largest 3-dB quality factor ($Q_{3dB}$); b) Admittance response ($Y_{12}$) and zoomed-in view of the peak region (blue box) of the S2MR showcasing the largest mechanical quality factor ($Q_m$) and electromechanical coupling ($k_t^2$) for different levels of input power ($P_{in}$). } \label{fig:power}
elements.sent-2411-354: The fabricated devices are characterized under laboratory conditions ($T$ = 293 K) with 150 \si{\micro\meter GSG probes in a two-port configuration to extract their characteristics frequency response.}
elements.sent-2411-355: Scattering ($S-$) parameters are measured with a vector network analyzer (Keysight P5008A) and later converted into admittance ($Y-$) parameters via software.
elements.sent-2411-356: The equivalent circuit parameters are extracted by fitting the transmission admittance ($Y_{12}$) response to a Modified Butterworth-Van Dyke (MBVD) equivalent model \cite{larson_modified_2000 .}
elements.sent-2411-357: The admittance of the device exhibiting the largest 3-dB quality factor ($Q_{3dB}$) is reported in Fig.~\ref{fig:device a, demonstrating a $Q_{3dB}$ of 277, a motional quality factor of $Q_m$, and $k_t^2$ of 4\%, for an overall Figure of Merit ($FOM \approx Q_m \cdot k_t^2$) slightly exceeding 14.}
elements.sent-2411-358: Power handling characterization is performed on a separate device, most closely matched to 50~$\Omega$, possessing similar quality factor ($Q_m$ = 380 and $Q_{3dB}$ = 216) and the highest measured coupling ($k_t^2 = 4.5\%$) and Figure of Merit ($FOM$ = 17).
elements.sent-2411-359: Fig.~\ref{fig:device b shows the admittance response as the input power is swept from 0 dBm to the maximum power permitted by the setup (20 dBm), and then returned to the baseline of 0 dBm.}
elements.sent-2411-360: A close-up of the resonant peak reveals minimal admittance degradation across the investigated input power range and no permanent deterioration of the quality factor upon returning to baseline.
elements.sent-2411-361: A first-order Temperature Coefficient of Frequency ($TCF_1$) of -40~ppm/K is finally inferred from temperature testing between 293 and 393~K \cite{giribaldi_compact_2024 .}
elements.sent-2411-362: To the best of the authors’ knowledge, S2MRs exhibit the highest Key Performance Indicators (KPIs) among solidly mounted resonators in the K$_u$ band, paving the way for the monolithic integration of nanoacoustic devices with high-power electronics on SiC for commercial, military, and harsh-environment applications requiring sharp frequency selectivity and high power handling.. \bibliographystyle{ieeetr.}
elements.sent-2411-363: This letter reports on Solidly-Mounted Bidimensional Mode Resonators (S2MRs) exploiting a highly-optimized Sezawa mode in 30\% Scandium-doped Aluminum Nitride (ScAlN) on Silicon Carbide (SiC) and operating near 16 GHz.
elements.sent-2411-364: Experimental results demonstrate mechanical quality factors ($Q_m$) as high as 380, Bode quality factors ($Q_{Bode}$) approaching 500, electromechanical coupling coefficients ($k_t^2$) of 4.5\%, an overall Figure of Merit ($FOM = Q_m \cdot k_t^2$) exceeding 17, and power handling greater than 20 dBm for devices closely matched to 50~$\Omega$.
elements.sent-2411-365: To the best of the authors' knowledge, S2MRs exhibit the highest Key Performance Indicators (KPIs) among solidly mounted resonators in the K$_u$-band, paving the way for the integration of nanoacoustic devices on fast substrates with high-power electronics, tailored for military and harsh-environment applications.
elements.sent-2411-366: Acoustic MEMS devices, such as Surface Acoustic Wave (SAW) \cite{takai_high-performance_2019 \cite{kimura \cite{LN and Film Bulk Acoustic Resonators (FBARs) \cite{ruby_decade_2011}\cite{hagelauer_microwave_2023 , represent the state-of-the-art solutions in the field, offering high selectivity and low insertion loss ($IL$) \cite{vetury_xbaw_2022 .}}}}}
elements.figure-2411-007: \centerline{\includegraphics[width=\columnwidth]{Figures/Fig1.png}} \caption{a) Cross-section of the proposed slow-on-fast solidly mounted bidimensional mode resonator (S2MR). The S2MR is implemented on thin film Scandium-doped Aluminum Nitride (ScAlN) on bulk 6H Silicon Carbide (SiC); b) Von Mises equivalent stress ($\sigma_{Mises}$ and $x-$ and $z-$ stress tensors ($\sigma_{xx}$ and $\sigma_{zz}$, respectively) according to COMSOL\textregistered~Finite Element Analysis (FEA) simulations; c) Displacement magnitude ($d$) and displacements in the $x-$ and $z-$ directions ($u$ and $w$, respectively) according to COMSOL\textregistered~FEA simulations. Periodic mechanical and electrical boundary conditions (BCs) are applied to the lateral edges of the structure, while free BCs are selected for the ScAlN top and SiC bottom surfaces.} \label{fig:enter-label}
elements.figure-2411-008: \centerline{\includegraphics[width=\columnwidth]{Figures/Fig2.png}} \caption{a) COMSOL\textregistered~FEA simulated electromechanical coupling ($k_t^2$) and propagation velocity at resonance ($v_{p_s}$) as a function of normalized piezoelectric film thickness ($h$) over acoustic wavelength ($\lambda$) for Scandium-doping concentrations ($Sc_{\%}$) ranging between 0 and 40\%. Aluminum (Al) top electrodes with a coverage ($c$) of 50\% and normalized metal thickness ($t_m$) over $\lambda$ of 0.125 are assumed. Higher Sc concentrations soften the material, hence increasing the coupling and reducing the propagation velocity; b) $k_t^2$ and $v_{p_s}$ as a function of $h/\lambda$ for normalized Al $t_m/\lambda$ ranging between 0.075 and 0.225, $Sc_{\%}$ of 30\%, and 50\% IDT coverage; c) Contour plot of $k_t^2$ as a function of $h/\lambda$ and $t_m/\lambda$ for 50\% coverage Al IDTs and 30\% Sc-doping. A unique local maximum for $h/\lambda$ = 0.65 and $t_m/\lambda$ = 0.125 exists. FEA simulated Von Mises stress distributions for $h/\lambda$ = 0.6 and $t_m/\lambda$ ranging between 0.025 and 0.325, highlighting the electrode-induced stress shift towards the piezoelectric-metal interface when thicker IDTs are adopted. A $t_m/\lambda$ ratio of 0.125 ensure maximum coupling between the electric field and the stress while preserving most of the elastic strain energy within the piezoelectric layer. } \label{fig:optmize}
elements.sent-2411-367: In this work, 30\%-doped AlN-seeded ScAlN, 6H-SiC, and patterned aluminum-silicon-copper (AlSiCu) with a 50\% metallization ratio ($c$) are implemented as the active layer, fast substrate, and IDT metal, respectively.
elements.figure-2411-009: \centerline{\includegraphics[width=\columnwidth]{Figures/Fig3.png}} \caption{a) Diagram of the micro-fabrication process adopted for the manufacturing of ScAlN S2MRs; Top to bottom: 1) ScAlN reactive sputtering on top of a ultra-thin AlN seed layer; 2) Top electrode patterning via electron beam (e-beam) lithography, aluminum-silicon-copper (AlSiCu) thermal evaporation, and lift-off; 3) Pads and interconnects patterning via direct laser lithography, gold (Au) e-beam evaporation, and lift-off; b) SEM image of the sputtered 30\%-doped ScAlN thin film surface, reporting low Abnormally Oriented Grains (AOGs) density; c) X-Ray Diffraction (XRD) Full-Width-Half-Maximum (FWHM) diagram, highlighting excellent crystallinity; and d) SEM image of a fabricated S2MR operating around 16 GHz. The reported device has an electrode aperture ($L_e$) of 5 \si{\micro\metre}, and acoustic wavelength ($\lambda$) of 400 nm, 120 finger pairs ($N_f$), and a finger width ($\lambda$/4) of 100 nm.} \label{fig:device}
elements.sent-2411-368: The results show an almost AOG-free top surface ($AOG_{\%}$ = 0.15\% and $AOG_{avg}$ = 137~nm) and a Rocking Curve (RC) of 1.5\textdegree~(Figs.~\ref{fig:device b-c).}
elements.sent-2411-369: A 50 nm thin film of aluminum-silicon-copper (AlSiCu) is deposited through thermal evaporation, followed by an overnight lift-off in 1165 Microposit remover.
elements.sent-2411-370: AlSiCu is adopted in place of pure aluminum due to its increased resistance to electromigration \cite{braunovic_electrical_2017 .}
elements.sent-2411-371: An SEM image of a fabricated devices is reported in Fig.~\ref{fig:device d.}
elements.figure-2411-010: \centerline{\includegraphics[width=\columnwidth]{Figures/Fig4.png}} \caption{a) Admittance response ($Y_{12}$) and phase ($\theta$) of a fabricated S2MR operating around 16 GHz and exhibiting the largest 3-dB quality factor ($Q_{3dB}$). The device is fit to a Modified Butterworth-Van Dyke (MBVD) model, which is reported in the inset. The 3-dB quality factor is calculated as $Q_{3dB}$ = $f_s/BW_{3dB}$, while the electromechanical coupling is calculated as $k_t^2 = \pi/2 \cdot f_s/f_p \cdot 1/\tan(\pi/2 \cdot f_s/f_p)$; b) Smith chart representation of the measured $S_{11}$ reflection coefficient parameter before (red line) and after (blue line) matching for Bode $Q$ extraction and inferred matched and smoothed $Q_{Bode}$ as a function of frequency; c) Admittance response ($Y_{12}$) and zoomed-in view of the peak region (blue box) of the S2MR showcasing the largest mechanical quality factor ($Q_m$) and electromechanical coupling ($k_t^2$) for different levels of applied input power ($P_{in}$). Motional parameters are reported for $P_{in}$~=~0~dBm. The legend details the tests as they were conducted, with the black dashed line indicating a return to baseline; and d) Temperature Coefficient of Frequency (TCF) testing for device R8C5 and applied temperature ($T_a$) ranging between 20\textdegree C and 125\textdegree C. } \label{fig:power}
elements.sent-2411-372: The admittance of the device exhibiting the largest 3-dB quality factor ($Q_{3dB}$) is reported in Fig.~\ref{fig:power a, demonstrating a $Q_{3dB}$ of 277, a motional quality factor of $Q_m$, and $k_t^2$ of 4\%, for an overall Figure of Merit ($FOM \approx Q_m \cdot k_t^2$) slightly exceeding 14.}
elements.sent-2411-373: A Bode quality factor ($Q_{Bode}$) of 500 is extracted from the same device (Fig.~\ref{fig:power b).}
elements.sent-2411-374: Fig.~\ref{fig:power c show the admittance response as the applied input power is swept from 0 dBm to the maximum power permitted by the setup (20~dBm), and then returned to the baseline of 0 dBm.}
elements.sent-2411-375: A close-up of the resonant peak reveals minimal admittance degradation across the investigated input power range and no deterioration of the quality factor upon returning to baseline.
elements.sent-2411-376: A first-order Temperature Coefficient of Frequency ($TCF_1$) of -95 ppm/K is finally inferred from temperature testing ($T_a$) between 293 and 318~K (Fig.~\ref{fig:power d).}
elements.sent-2411-377: To the best of the authors’ knowledge, S2MRs exhibit the highest Key Performance Indicators (KPIs) among solidly mounted resonators in the K$_u$-band, paving the way for the monolithic integration of nanoacoustic devices with high-power electronics on SiC for commercial, military, and harsh-environment applications requiring sharp frequency selectivity and high power handling.. \bibliographystyle{ieeetr.}
